  • Step 6: Lastly, as the name of the series suggests, we want to have some names that may be trading cheaper in comparison to their historical valuation. So, we select the top ten names with the highest discount. However, they could be trading cheap for just the wrong reasons, so we need to be careful that they meet our other quality criteria.

From the above steps, we now have a total of 70 names in our final consideration. However, the following stocks appeared more than once:

Stocks that appeared two times: ARE, AU, CI, COKE, GE, IBKR, LYB, OC, TJX, V, and WIT (11 duplicates).

Stocks that appeared three times: CIB, HWM (4 duplicates).

After removing eight duplicates, we are left with 55 (70-15) names.

Since we still have a very large list, we will remove names that have a low yield and not very great dividend growth. So, if the dividend yield was less than < 2.5% and the dividend growth (average of 5-year and 10-year) was less than 10%, we would rather remove them at this stage.

So, we remove WMT and JPM. After removing them, we are left with 53 names.

Since there are still multiple names in each industry segment, we will keep a maximum of two or three names (from the top) from any one segment.

On this basis, we remove a total of 13 names: WLK, BAH, WIT, MSFT, ACN, MPWR, NKE, COKE, TRGP, CI, NVO, LLY, and NDSN.

After we remove these 15 (2+13) names, we are left with 40 (55-15) names.

Final Step: Narrowing Down To Just Five Companies

In this step, we construct three separate lists of five stocks each, with different sets of goals, dividend income, and risk levels.

The lists are:

1) Relatively Safe (Low-yield) Dividend list,

2) Moderately High Dividend List,

3) Ultra High Dividend List, and

4) A combined list of the above three (duplicates removed).

Out of the top 40, we make our judgment calls to make these three lists, so basically, the selections are based on our research and perceptions. So, while most of the filtering until now was based on automated criteria, the last step is pretty much subjective. We try to make each of the three lists highly diversified among various sectors and industry segments, and try to ensure that the safety of dividends matches the overall risk profile of the group. We certainly encourage readers to do further research on the highlighted names.

Nonetheless, here are our three final lists for this month:

Final A-List (Relatively Safe Income):

Criteria for this group:

  • The average yield for the group is between 2.75% and 4.25%.
  • At least three or four names with an "A" rating (from S&P)
  • Solid track record of dividends and growth.

Average yield: 3.63%

  • (INTU)
  • (ADP)
  • (CMCSA)
  • (AEG)
  • (CVX).

Table-1A: A-LIST (Conservative Income)

We think this set of five companies (in the A-List) would form a solid diversified group of dividend companies that would be appealing to income-seeking and conservative investors, including retirees and near-retirees. The average yield is 3.63%, which is more than 3x of what the S&P 500 pays. The average dividend history is 26 years. Also, all five companies have an excellent rating of "A-" or higher. If you must need higher dividends, consider the B-List or the C-List, presented later.

As usual, we will provide a brief synopsis of each of the companies selected here; however, we will not go into too many details, as that will make the article unduly long.

INTU (Intuit Inc):

The current dividend yield of Intuit is very low at 0.76%, so it does not serve the purpose of retirees who may depend on dividends for their income. But the reason we include it in the A-list is due to its potential for dividend growth. The dividend growth has been 15% (or more) for 3, 5, and 10-year periods. Its major premium products include TurboTax and QuickBooks.

Intuit appears to be firing on all cylinders and has achieved 20% revenue growth in the last quarter and 16% growth on a full-year basis, both beating management's own guidance. The operating margins have also been expanding nicely. There are some risks and competitive threats from free programs, such as the IRS's "Direct File" program. However, it poses only minimal risk to Intuit's business.

ADP (Automatic Data Processing):

ADP's yield is relatively low at 2.4%, but has gone up considerably in recent times due to a bit of price correction. It is often considered a favorite of dividend investors, given the company's stability and reliability of the dividend and above-average dividend growth. It is relatively rare to find this company trading at cheaper valuations, except during deep market corrections. Morningstar rates the stock price just about "fairly valued." So, the shares are not cheap either, but still 10-15% below the 52-week high.

For dividend investors, the company offers a 50-year record of consistently growing dividends. The last increase was a 10% increase. The past 3-year and 5-year dividend growth has been impressive at roughly 14% and 11%, respectively. Even though the yield is relatively low, we think high growth should compensate for the low yield in the long run. It is being included in the A-list for its exceptional record of growing dividends and excellent credit rating.

CVX (CHEVRON):

CVX needs no introduction as an oil major and frequently takes a spot in our A-list due to its relatively safe, moderately high dividend, and excellent credit rating. It is probably one of the best investments in its sector for stable income. However, earnings and the share price of the company are obviously tied to the movement of the oil prices, which can fluctuate widely based on supply/demand issues and geopolitical situations of the day. Chevron is known to be a low-cost producer with its breakeven price in the low to mid-40s.

Similar to previous months, we are including CVX in the A-list for reasons stated above. CVX currently offers a highly attractive dividend yield of 4.5% plus. The payout ratio is currently a bit high on an EPS basis but reasonable on a cash flow basis. The dividend growth has been a bit tepid over the years, but reasonable at 6% plus, considering the starting yield. The company maintains an excellent credit rating of "AA" from S&P, which shows its financial strength.

CMCSA (Comcast):

Comcast is a Communications and Media company. The company is dealing with a consistent decline in its traditional cable business, but that is being compensated for by the growth in the broadband and connectivity business.

That said, the company is struggling to grow its top line. Overall, the company's revenue grew by just 0.2% last year. However, the company is still highly profitable with 72% gross profit margins and 31% EBITDA margins, which are impressive by any standard. The business also generates a ton of cash, even though it has a substantial amount of debt of $99 billion, similar to its peers. The company is expanding its theme park business and is actively considering the acquisition of Warner Bros.

In spite of a lack of growth, the company appears to be highly undervalued (trading at just 6.5x earnings), and is currently offering a very attractive yield of nearly 5%. Only patient income investors should consider this, as it may take a while for the price to recover.

AEG (Aegon)

The company provides financial planning and wealth management services, including life insurance, retirement solutions, and asset management services. The company's major share of business comes from the U.S. and U.K., and to a lesser extent from Spain, Portugal, China, and Brazil.

The company has been performing very well in the recent past and has met most of its targets. The company is also considering moving its domicile to the U.S., as the Americas contribute roughly two-thirds of the total revenue. The dividend growth has been massive at nearly 30% just during the last 3 years. The current yield is very attractive at 5.5%. Payout ratio is reasonably low at 46% and the company has an excellent credit rating.

Final B-List (High Yield, Moderately Safe):

Criteria for this group:

  • Yield for the group between 4.75% to 6.50%.
  • At least two names with 'A-' (or above) rated.
  • Above-average track record of dividends and growth.

Average yield: 6.21%

  • (ADP)
  • (CMCSA)
  • (ARE)
  • (CVX)
  • (ARCC).

Note 1: Very often, we include a few low-risk stocks in the B-list and C-list. Also, oftentimes, a stock can appear in multiple lists. This is done on purpose. We try to make each of our lists fairly diversified among different sectors/industry segments of the economy. We try to include a few of the highly conservative names in the high-yield list to make the overall group much safer.

Table-1B: B-LIST (High Yield)

In the B-List, the overall risk profile of the group becomes slightly elevated compared to the A-List. That said, the group (as a whole) will likely provide safe dividends for many years. This list offers an average yield of 6.2% and an average of 30 years of dividend history and 19 years (average) of consecutive growth.

Some of our high-yield selections often repeat in the B-list and C-list.

ARE (Alexandria Real Estate Equities):

ARE is a life-sciences-focused REIT (Real Estate Investment Trust) company. It is headquartered in California, but it has life sciences prime property assets all across the U.S. It has nearly 750+ sector-leading clients that drive stable and long-duration cash flows. It is currently facing headwinds due to overbuilding in the sector and high vacancy rates. The share price has lost nearly 50% in the last 10 years, and the price to FFO (funds from operations) is currently 10x, the lowest since the financial crisis in 2008.

On the positive side, the company has continued solid leasing volume of one million rentable square feet, marking the fifth straight quarter with leasing volume exceeding 1 million sq ft.

Also, on the positive side, it is one of the best companies with highly experienced management in its sector and owns high-quality assets. It has a solid balance sheet with high liquidity and very few debt maturities in the near term. To income investors, it currently offers a very nice yield of nearly 10% on the current prices, with much of the downside already built in. The share price already appears to be at the bottom, and the further downside appears limited, but it is nearly impossible to predict price movements.

All that said, this is our most risky pick in the B-List. As such, the dividend appears safe with a payout ratio of only 60% based on FFO. However, there is a small chance that it would cut the dividends in 2026. Even though a cut is priced in the market price, it will still push the prices down if and when that happens.

NOTE/UPDATE: After we submitted the article (and scheduled for publication), "ARE" announced a roughly 45% dividend cut to $0.72 a share per quarter. We expected a cut, but more like 25%. The stock lost nearly 10% on the news, but the slide is likely to continue for some more time. Now the yield stands at about 6% but will likely go up with some more correction in the price. The dividend cut will likely be good for the long-term health of the company, but not so good for income investors in the short to medium term. Please use due diligence.

ARCC (Ares Capital):

We are including Ares Capital in the B-list and the C-list. ARCC is one of the largest BDCs (Business Development Companies) in terms of market capitalization and net asset value. The company invests in small and medium-sized companies and has a diversified portfolio of investments in over 500 companies. Besides, ARCC has a proven record of performing reasonably well both in high and low-interest-rate environments. Pricing and valuation-wise, it trades at a slight premium (which has come down recently) for being one of the largest in the BDC arena, and its stable dividend.

It generates a solid amount of NII (net Investment income), which currently exceeds well above the $1.2 billion mark on an annual basis. ARCC currently offers a highly attractive yield of roughly 9.3%.

Final C-LIST (Yield-Hungry, Less Safe):

Criteria for this group:

  • The average yield for the group is between 7.0% to 8.0%
  • All names with investment-grade ratings.
  • Some potential for growth.

Average yield: 8.64%

  • (CMCSA)
  • (MPLX)
  • (ARE)
  • (ARCC)
  • (LYB).

Notes:

Note 1: Oftentimes, a stock can appear in multiple lists. We try to include one or two conservative names in the high-yield list to make the overall group much safer.

Table-1C: C-LIST (Yield-Hungry, Elevated Risk)

NOTE: MPLX is structured as a partnership (not a corporation) and issues a K-1 tax form instead of the usual 1099-Div. Please use due diligence.

=====

It is apparent from the selections that this list (C-List) is for yield-hungry DGI investors. The yield goes up to 8.6%. However, please note that this list is not for conservative investors. As you can see, the average credit rating of this set of companies is much lower than the A-List or even the B-List. Dividends are moderately safe in this list, but not nearly as safe as in the A-List or even the B-list. At least a couple of companies in this list come with an elevated level of risk, as they are turnaround stories. We urge due diligence to determine if it would suit your personal situation. Nothing comes for free, so there will be more risk involved with this group. Even then, it's a diversified group spread among five different industry segments.

MPLX (MPLX LP):

For the C-List, the dividend yield of Chevron is somewhat low. So, we replace it with MPLX LP, a highly attractive name from the midstream energy industry, mainly to elevate the yield. Please note that the company has a Partnership structure and issues a K-1 tax form (partnership income) instead of the usual 1099 in case of regular dividends.

We have covered MPLX multiple times in our previous articles in this monthly series. Hence, we will skip the details here. MPLX regularly features on our B-list and C-list due to its stable dividend growth and high yield. There are other good alternatives, such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) or Energy Transfer (ET).

LYB (LyondellBasell Industries, N.V.):

LYB deals in petrochemicals and commodity chemicals and has a large manufacturing presence in North America, which provides it access to relatively cheaper natural-gas-based feedstocks even though it is headquartered in the Netherlands. The petrochemicals sector has been facing cyclical headwinds in general, as well as pricing pressures from new production capacity in China.

The company appears to be undervalued currently. It has been trading at attractive levels for some time due to macro headwinds, but the prices have undergone further correction in recent times due to some fear of cutting dividends. However, the company is dealing with economic pressures by cutting costs, reducing capital spending, and moving further production to North America. The current yield is very tempting at 11% plus. Please note that there is a moderate to high risk that the company comes under further pressure and cuts its dividends, which it has avoided steadfastly so far (unlike its peers). However, even if the dividend is cut, it is likely to remain north of 7% to 8%, but the price may come under further pressure; however, most of the downside is priced in. Considering these risks, only patient and risk-tolerant investors should consider it.

We are including LYB in our C-list because of the high yield and undervaluation. Morningstar currently assigns a five-star valuation rating to LYB. Morningstar's own fair-value estimates for LYB have come down in the last year; even then, the current market price is nearly 50% below their estimates. That said, we must keep in mind that Morningstar's fair value estimate is not static, and it can change when new data emerges.

Note: We would like to caution that each company comes with certain risks and concerns. Sometimes, these risks are real, but other times, they may be a bit overblown and temporary. So, it's always recommended to do further research and due diligence.

What If We Were to Combine the Three Lists?

If we combine the three lists and remove the duplicates (because of combining), we would be left with nine names.

Two-time duplicates: ADP, ARCC, ARE, and CVX (4 duplicates)

Three-time duplicates: CMCSA (2 duplicates).

After removing these SIX duplicates, we are left with nine names.

The combined list is highly diversified in as many as eight different industry segments. However, there may be multiple names for some of the sectors. The stats for the group of 9 are as follows:

Average yield: 6.27%

Average discount (from 52WK High): -22%

Average 5-yr dividend growth: 19%

Average Payout Ratio: 67% (on the higher side due to LYB).

Average Total Quality Score: 66.6.

Table 2:

Conclusion:

In this article, we have presented three groups of dividend-paying stocks (five each) with different goals in mind to suit the varying needs of a wider audience. Even though the risk profile of each group is different, each group in itself is fairly balanced and diversified.

This month, the first group yields 3.6%, while the second group elevates the yield to 6.2%. We also presented a C-list for yield-hungry investors with an 8.6% yield. The combined group (all three lists combined with duplication removed) offers an even more diversified group with nine positions and a 6.3% yield.

data.title: Orla Mining: A Consistent Per Share Grower At A Reasonable Price
data.description:
All figures are in United States dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground. MTPA = million tonnes per annum. FS/DFS = Definitive Feasibility Study.

Q3 Production & Sales

Orla Mining (ORLA)(OLA:CA) released its Q3'25 results this week, reporting quarterly production of ~79,600 ounces of gold (+82% year-over-year), while gold sales increased 106% year-over-year to ~78,900 ounces. Higher production was related to the acquisition of the Musselwhite Mine in Canada earlier this year for a very attractive price (~$430/oz on total resources for a producing asset), with production from Musselwhite coming in at ~57,600 ounces in the period.

Unfortunately, this was partially offset by a softer quarter at Camino Rojo Oxides, where lower grades were stacked following a pit-wall event on the north wall of the pit in late July. Importantly, there were no environmental impacts, no injuries or equipment damage, and the movement was detected early by its pit-wall monitoring systems. Further, no ore was lost or sterilized as part of the mine plan, with ~9 million tonnes of mostly oxidized material to be mined, crushed, and stacked on its heap leach pads during the remediation period.

Still, the result is that Orla is completing a 50-80 meter pushback with redesigned slopes at its #2 mine, affecting grades short-term (0.40 G/T of gold vs. 0.93 G/T of gold) at what is otherwise an above-average grade and extremely low-cost heap-leach asset (as shown below), with Camino Rojo's all-in sustaining costs roughly 50% below the industry average last year prior to this event ($806/oz).

Despite the weaker quarter at Camino Rojo (~22,100 ounces at $1,078/oz AISC) as guided, Musselwhite performed extremely well in its second full quarter under Orla's ownership, producing ~57,600 ounces at $1,697/oz AISC. It's important to note that AISC is elevated short-term because of higher sustaining capital expenditures vs. the LOM average, with ~$45 million spent in the past seven months (~$77 million annualized) vs. LOM average sustaining capital spend closer to ~$42 million in the 2025-2029 period.

It's also worth noting that mining costs were above plan due to increased lateral development (2,700+ meters); processing costs were elevated in early Q3'25 because of contractor maintenance/crusher system work; and Musselwhite will see cost improvements starting in late 2025 and going forward as it fully moves away from existing Newmont systems.

Further, Orla shared that recent investments in new UG mobile equipment and its preventative maintenance program are driving productivity gains and resulting in improved working conditions, with this mine somewhat neglected under Newmont in terms of investment in exploration/fleet upgrades given that it was a non-core asset for a company of its scale (~200,000 ounces vs. ~6.0 million ounce production profile).

As for operating metrics, Orla mined ~325,900 tonnes at Musselwhite, with ~329,600 tonnes processed at an average grade of 5.87 G/T of gold, generating ~$193 million in revenue, with over 70% of revenue now coming from Canada vs. 100% from Mexico at this time last year. However, this throughput rate is still well below the 1.5 MTPA capacity at the mill, pointing to further upside if Orla can fill the mill.

As for recent developments, mine development, UG drilling, and ventilation-upgrade projects continued with completion expected by year-end/early 2026, and the company has commissioned a new triple-stack stope at Redwings in the upper portion of the mine, contributing to higher ore tonnes looking through to year-end.

This is an important point because although slightly lower grade (~5 G/T of gold in the upper portion of the mine vs. 6/7 G/T of gold as it goes deeper), tonnes pulled from the upper mine can be incremental to feed, allowing Musselwhite to leverage excess mill capacity and process closer to 1.2 - 1.3 MTPA vs. capacity of 1.5 MTPA and where Newmont was running the mine at closer to 1.0 MTPA, or 30% below capacity.

2025 Guidance

As for guidance, Orla has an exceptional track record of beating guidance with three consecutive beats in a row vs. its guidance midpoint with an average beat of 16.8%, or ~17,700 ounces on average per year. Unfortunately, the pit wall event at Camino Rojo derailed its plans to make it a 4-year streak, with Orla having to revise guidance from 280,000 to 300,000 ounces (290,000 ounce midpoint) to 265,000 to 285,000 ounces (275,000 ounce midpoint) earlier this year.

However, with ~205,000 ounces YTD, Orla is sitting at ~75% of its updated guidance and ~71% of its original guidance, and the company noted it expects to finish near the upper end of its production guidance. Assuming ~279,000 ounces this year, it would still represent a miss vs. its initial guidance midpoint. However, I would argue that is a very solid comeback considering the challenges this year (pit wall event) and a testament to this team's conservatism when a setback at one of its two assets that significantly impacts its grade profile still leaves it on track to meet the low end of original guidance (280,000 ounces - 300,000 ounces).

It's also worth noting that, even assuming we do see a ~4% miss on its initial guidance midpoint, Orla will still have the top spot in terms of beat/miss % vs. its peers on a 4-year average basis with a 7%+ average beat on a 4-year average basis.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

“With pit stabilization at Camino Rojo progressing well and integration at Musselwhite advancing smoothly, both operations are performing well. We are now on track to achieve the high end of our revised production guidance.”

– Jason Simpson, President and CEO, Orla Mining

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

To be clear, I am not applauding the ability to deliver at the top of a downward revised guidance range. However, I think it's encouraging to see the company sticking to its conservative roots despite having to play catch-up, and it provides a positive read-through for 2026 given that Orla will likely do as it typically does, which is guide conservatively up front and then trounce guidance, providing upside surprises vs. analyst estimates that often allow it to significantly outperform its peers.

Financial Results

As for Orla's financial results, it is a completely different company following the Musselwhite acquisition and the tailwind of record gold prices. Revenue jumped 177% year-over-year to ~$275 million, operating cash flow surged to ~$123 million (+134% year-over-year), and free cash flow soared to ~$90 million, with free cash flow per share coming in at ~$0.27 vs. $0.14 in the year-ago period.

Importantly, this was despite delivering ~12,100 ounces or ~15% of production, into gold pre-pays at $2,912/oz, impacting its average realized gold, which came in at $3,417/oz but would have been above $3,500/oz if not for these pre-pays. As it stands, ~113,000 ounces remain to be delivered into prepays over the next 28 months, representing less than 20% of production in the period.

Given the strong financial performance, Orla ended the quarter with ~$330 million in cash and cash equivalents and ~$360 million in liquidity. As for net debt, Orla is rapidly deleveraging, with it now having just ~$210 million in net debt based on its $90 million RCF balance (August 28th, 2027 maturity), $100 million term loan (February 26th, 2028 maturity) and prepayment obligations. Based on current gold prices, Orla will have no problem paying these down well ahead of maturity, with the company set to generate ~$390 million in free cash flow next year even at a $3,700/oz gold price, including the impact of prepays.

Costs & Margins

Moving to costs and margins, Orla was previously the lowest-cost producer in the sector, but Musselwhite has obviously changed its cost profile. That said, I think this was the right move to diversify into a more attractive jurisdiction and shed its single-asset producer status, given that its third producing mine is still two years away from heading into commercial production in Nevada. As for AISC, it was above the industry average at $1,641/oz in Q3'25, but was impacted by elevated sustaining capital at both assets and should head back below $1,500/oz next year, further improving to ~$1,400/oz once South Railroad is online in H2'28.

Fortunately, the record gold price more than offset the increase in costs from adding a larger but higher-cost mine in Musselwhite, with AISC margins coming in at $1,766/oz (52.0%) vs. $1,757/oz (70.9%) in the year-ago period. Obviously, the margin hit may not be to some investors' liking, but this was inevitable as there simply aren't many sub $800/oz AISC operations out there, and I think acquiring Musselwhite was a brilliant move at the price paid, especially given the exploration/production upside here.

Recent Developments

South Railroad Permitting & Timeline

As for recent developments, investors will be pleased to know that South Railroad (Orla's third future mine) remains on track to receive its Record of Decision by mid-2026, setting up construction next year and first production in early 2028. Notably, South Railroad was moved to FAST-41 inclusion to streamline and de-risk the permitting process, and the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers 404 permit application covering wetland/surface disturbance impacts was submitted in August and deemed administratively complete with the public-comment period closed in late September with no significant issues and the BLM-NEPA public comment for the project's Environmental Assessment closed in mid-September with the largest category of submissions being letters of support for the project.

As for state progress, Orla has received Class I and II Air Operating permits, and water-related applications are being reviewed by the NDWR.

Orla noted that the next steps include an updated Feasibility Study to be completed by year-end, including tighter cost estimates with actual vendor pricing and detailed engineering to de-risk assumptions around the project. Originally, South Railroad was expected to produce ~200,000 ounces in Year 2 and 3 in peak years and ~152,000 ounces in its first four years at ~$950/oz AISC with an upfront capex bill of ~$190 million. I think it's more conservative to assume ~$1,250/oz AISC and an upfront capex bill of ~$285 million, but this still makes the project incredibly attractive given a barely one-year payback on an 8-year mine life and what I believe will ultimately be a 15-20 year mine life.

The latter view is because this is an asset with no shortage of oxide targets, clear evidence from its neighbor, Nevada Gold Mines, that there is enormous sulfide potential at depth below the closer-to-surface oxide mineralization, and the fact that we've already seen solid exploration success at Pinion SB (southern extension of Pinion Pit), and depth extensions at Dark Star, the higher-grade starter pit on the project.

As for the timeline, it sounds like there is some room to beat the early 2028 target, but Orla is erring on the conservative side, which is the smart move given that while construction timelines are in its control, permitting is not, even if things continue to progress better than expected.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

"In terms of the production date, what I'd like to comment about that is I will protect Andrew and his team and their build construction schedule. We are a company that like to deliver on our cost and timeline objectives. And it really all comes down to how quickly we can get started in the spring.

The current timeline as published on the federal website shows towards the end of Q2, final permitting Record of Decision. If that can be accelerated earlier into the spring, there are prospects for us to pull that first gold into the tail end of '27. But officially, we are on the record saying that we will pour first gold early in 2028 and have effectively an almost full year of production in 2028.

If things change with the permitting and things continue to get accelerated as we've seen over the last 6 months, then we'll update the market."

- Orla Mining Q3'25 Conference Call

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Musselwhite

As for its Musselwhite Mine, Orla remains very bullish on exploration upside here above the current ~6-year mine plan that supports ~202,000 ounces per annum on average with sub $1,500/oz AISC more conservatively. In fact, the company has gone on record saying that if its aggressive 2-year exploration program is successful, Musselwhite could grow towards 300,000+ ounces, a massive boost to production that could be realized by the end of this decade.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

"But focusing on Musselwhite, and the potential expansion there, really this exploration that we planned over the 24-month period will lead to an investment decision to improve the material handling system. What that could do for our Musselwhite asset is increase the production annually from 200,000 ounces to potentially over 300,000 ounces."

- Jason Simpson, CEO of Orla Mining, Bloomerg

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Fortunately, exploration has kicked off with success, with impressive hits in current production areas that should support resource/reserve growth over the next two years, including (true widths):

Redwings Zone:

  • 11.7 meters at 6.89 G/T of gold
  • 4.1 meters at 12.2 G/T of gold
  • 13.2 meters at 6.68 G/T of gold
  • 11.8 meters at 6.3 G/T of gold

Lynx Zone:

  • 13.0 meters at 15.8 G/T of gold
  • 6.1 meters at 27.2 G/T of gold
  • 7.3 meters at 9.87 G/T of gold

PQE Zone:

  • 0.9 meters at 67.8 G/T of gold
  • 2.1 meters at 14.0 G/T of gold

In more exciting news, drilling 1.6 kilometers along strike from current mineral reserves hit 4.1 meters at 15.1 G/T of gold in mother hole #2, suggesting potential for material resource growth if this area shows continuity. Meanwhile, assays are pending for drilling from mother hole #3 that would represent a 2.0 kilometer extension from the main mine. It's also important to note that these are not desperate Hail Mary drill holes that are simply hoping to hit anything.

In fact, there is clear evidence of thicker and higher-grade mineralization ~1 kilometer from current mining areas, with Goldcorp/Newmont hitting 16.8 meters at 9.29 G/T of gold, 13.4 meters at 16.2 G/T of gold, and 9.1 meters at 23.4 G/T of gold in 2018/2019 drilling.

Shareholder Returns

Finally, on shareholder returns, record free cash flow generation (Q3'25: ~$90 million), even with Camino Rojo chewing through higher-strip and lower-grade material and while delivering into prepays and at lower gold prices ($3,400/oz), has the company looking at possible shareholder returns. If put in place, this would certainly separate Orla from its smaller 200,000 to 400,000-ounce producer peers, with very few of them having respectable base dividends in place.

At this time, it's too hard to factor in what the company might consider paying when it has a growth project on deck where construction starts in less than a year. However, even at 15% of free cash flow at a more conservative $3,000/oz gold price assumption (~$38 million of ~$256 million in 2026 at $3,000/oz gold), we could see Orla paying a dividend of $0.10+ per share annualized.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

"And then finally, it's important and is an active conversation currently with the current gold price and the health of our balance sheet to begin graduating to return additional returns to investors, likely through a dividend. So, that conversation is active. And once the Board approves any changes to our capital allocation strategy, we'll announce it to the market."

- Orla Mining, Q3'25 Conference Call

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Valuation

Based on ~414 million fully diluted shares (includes potential dilution from convertible notes all warrants/options) and a share price of US$12.00, Orla trades at a market cap of ~$4.97 billion and an enterprise value of ~$5.18 billion (cash [-] gold prepays and principal on RCF and term loan). This may seem like an elevated valuation for a ~300,000-ounce producer, even if the bulk of production is coming from Canada/USA. However, it's important to note that Orla has an industry-leading growth profile and the potential to increase free cash flow to ~$906 million to ~$1.05 billion in 2029 at a $3,800/oz to $4,300/oz gold price assumption.

Hence, when factoring in this growth (which doesn't show up in its FY2026 EV/FCF estimates), Orla is actually one of the cheaper names sector-wide, especially on a quality-adjusted basis with a management team that has consistently over-delivered on promises and a company that boasts industry-leading per-share growth. Further, Orla will have not one, but two simple, low-cost, and low-capex heap-leach mines (South Railroad, USA and Camino Rojo Oxides, Mexico) and a large producing asset in Canada, Musselwhite, by 2029, further diversifying its cash flow and contributing to industry-leading free cash flow margins (lower cost operations vs. peers + relatively low capex).

It's also important to note that this ~60% growth in its production profile (base case) has material incremental upside on top of it if exploration success at depth continues at Musselwhite and Camino Rojo.

This is because Camino Rojo UG (which sits just below its currently producing oxide mine) is home to ~4.6 million gold-equivalent ounces and growing (gold-silver-zinc), which we'll get more information on next year with a PEA to see what this asset could look like from a scale standpoint. Meanwhile, Orla has been clear that if exploration success permits, investments will be made in material handling to fill the Musselwhite mill, which is currently operating at less than 70% of its total capacity. This would take production closer to ~300,000 ounces/year.

Hence, while the medium-term ambition is to grow into a 500,000+ ounce producer, there are two organic growth opportunities that could take production upwards of 600,000 ounces with additional optionality in Panama (Cerro Quema) that would take GEO production closer to 700,000 ounces (I have valued Cerro Quema at zero for conservatism).

The other key point, though, is that producers that rank in the upper echelon for quality (i.e., track record of per share growth, operational execution, delivery vs. promises) consistently trade at premium valuations, as is evidenced by Lundin Gold (OTCQX:LUGDF), a single-asset producer, trading at ~20x free cash flow or a similar multiple to companies like Agnico Eagle (AEM). And while this is undoubtedly partially attributed to the quality of the Fruta del Norte Mine, the same can be seen in Discovery Silver (OTCQX:DSVSF), another name with strong management and a history of impressive delivery that also trades at a large premium to peers.

Hence, while Orla Mining might appear fairly valued vs. a cluster of lower-priced peers, I think the one important piece missed by the market is that this isn't an average producer; it's a company with the best beat/miss track record of all of its producer peers the past three years by a wide margin among the 50+ producers I track. Further, until its Musselwhite acquisition, which provided scale and needed diversification, it was the lowest-cost producer in the sector, with trailing-three-year average all-in sustaining costs of $707/oz.

In summary, this is a rare case where management's ability to massively over-deliver on promises needs to be factored into the thesis here (and arguably is not priced in at all today at ~5.6x FY2028 EV/FCF estimates at a flat $3,800/oz gold price). Additionally, producers of this quality can be very difficult to buy, as we've seen with Lundin Gold (OTCQX:LUGDF), hence why I'm elated to have been able to get an opportunity to start a position in ORLA below US$9.50/share earlier last month, and it's a name where I trust management will continue to grow per-share value over time.

Further, and as is clear from the above charts, you are getting real leverage to the gold price with a company like ORLA that is growing per share metrics at breakneck speed with the benefit of strong backing (Fairfax Financial, Pierre Lassonde) to support future opportunities that might arise vs. names like Newmont (NEM), Kinross (KGC) and many others where even a higher gold price can only help so much as per share metrics wilt over time.

So, what's a fair value for the stock?

Using what I believe to be fair multiples of 10.0x cash flow and 1.2x P/NAV 7% discount rate for a dual-asset producer with the bulk of its NAV tied to Canada/United States and an industry-leading track record of delivery with phenomenal operational execution, I see an updated fair value for Orla Mining of US$14.60/share, pointing to a 21% upside from current levels after yesterday's rally (65% weighting to P/NAV, 35% to P/CF).

However, this assumes a conservative $3,700/oz and $3,450/oz gold price over the next two years, respectively, and what I believe to be a very conservative $2,900/oz gold price long-term. This fair value estimate also assigns zero value to its Cerro Quema Project in Panama, which is under arbitration, but where Franco-Nevada and First Quantum have suspended arbitration temporarily as they try to work with the government on a solution to restart Cobre Panama.

(*) While I don't place any value on Cerro Quema and it's unclear whether Orla would build or sell in a more supportive environment for mining in Panama, this is a solid little copper-gold project with the potential to produce ~80,000 ounces/year at sub $850/oz AISC (adjusted for inflation), similar to Camino Rojo Oxides. (*)

The other point worth noting is that Orla's cash flow estimates that inform its fair value estimate (35% weighting to P/CF estimates vs. 65% weighting to P/NAV) are weighed down medium-term by gold pre-pays,of which ~48,000 ounces are to be delivered next year (~15% of production) related to the Musselwhite acquisition. Hence, the cash flow potential for this business is far more significant looking out to 2028, but these pprepaysaffect its fair value estimate short-term.

The last point worth noting is that it's difficult to model the exploration upside for this portfolio, but, like Calibre (pre-acquisition), Orla is certainly one of the more aggressive drillers in the sub 1.0 million ounce producer space and has three massive land packages in prolific jurisdictions. This includes:

1) ~65,000 hectares at Musselwhite, an asset that has produced over 6 million ounces of gold 500 kilometers north of Thunder Bay in Ontario, Canada

2) ~21,000 hectares and the second-largest land position only to Newmont/Barrick on the Carlin Trend in Nevada at its South Carlin Project, with the Carlin Trend produced ~105 million ounces of gold to date

3) ~139,000 hectares in Zacatecas, Mexico, a state known for high-grade and highly productive gold deposits/mines, including Fresnillo's Juanicipio, Fresnillo and Saucito mines, Newmont's Penasquito, Agnico/Teck's San Nicolas, and Pan American's La Colorada Mine.

Long-Term Potential

As for long-term potential, Orla has the potential to generate ~$729 million ($3,300/oz gold), ~$906 million ($3,800/oz gold) and ~$1.05 billion ($4,200/oz gold) in free cash flow in 2029 once South Railroad is ramped up to full production and assuming no upside from Camino Rojo Sulphides and an expansion at Musselwhite,which have the potential to push production closer to 600,000 GEOs in 2030 (2029 estimates assume 517,000 ounces produced).

Even at what I believe to be a more than fair 14x FCF multiple for what would be a tri-asset producer with cash flow coming from Canada, the USA,and Mexico, this translates to a fair value of US$21.50 in 2028 and US$30.40 in 2029 at a $3,800/oz gold price assumption. Hence, I see significant long-term upside in Orla and I ultimately think it will find a way to grow this company into a 700,000+ ounce producer within the next 6-7 years, providing even greater returns for shareholders at this scale as a 4-mine company.

Technical Picture

Finally, as for its technical picture, ORLA has spent eight months consolidating with multiple tests of the US$13.00 region, suggesting the next sustained move through this resistance area has a higher probability of being a successful breakout. Meanwhile, the recent shake-out in the stock that was a rollercoaster ride for those chasing the breakout near US$14.00 only to suffer a ~32% correction in 15 days has significantly improved the technical setup and created a similar setup to the one before its large run with a breakout, deep pullback to undercut its 200-day moving average followed by a gap higher and new sustained uptrend after this shakeout.

To be very clear, the similarity in these two setups does not mean that they have to play out similarly by any means, and past performance is no guarantee of future returns. However, this is a very constructive setup in ORLA today and with one of the highest-quality producers on a track record standpoint pulling back below its 200-DMA below US$9.50, this setup was truly a gift to get long the stock with minimal downside risk with the opportunity to potentially ride a next leg higher in the stock.

Summary

Orla put together another solid quarter in Q3'25 and has rebounded strongly after the setback at Camino Rojo to put itself in a position to deliver just shy of its initial guidance. More importantly, though, the company continues to enjoy resounding exploration success across its portfolio, with new discoveries at South Carlin and major step-outs at Musselwhite suggesting both assets have much more in inventory than their current reserve lives suggest.

So, with aggressive exploration programs across three prolific land packages in jurisdictions home to elephant-sized deposits (Carlin Complex, Penasquito, and several 10+ million ounce deposits in northern Ontario), an industry-leading growth profile and Orla sitting at just 6x FY2028 EV/FCF estimates (flat $3,700/oz gold price assumption), I would view any sharp pullbacks in the stock as buying opportunities.

data.title: The Trade Desk: Potential Value Trap - Recovery Likely Lumpy And Prolonged (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I previously covered The Trade Desk, Inc. (TTD) in August 2025, discussing how its independent DSP story had not played out as expected, with Kokai/OpenPath yet to be accretive to its FQ2'25 performance and FQ3'25 guidance.

Despite the ongoing underperformance against other walled gardens/DSP players that had been reporting high double-digit growths, I had believed that the ad tech company remained compelling then, thanks to the cheaper valuations, the lack of debt, and the promising early Kokai results in FQ2'25 - resulting in my reiterated Buy rating.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am admitting defeat and downgrading the TTD stock to a Hold here, as my prior Buy ratings fail to perform to expectations, attributed to the intensifying competition, the decelerating growth profile across FQ3'25 results/FQ4'25 guidance, and the potential value trap.

TTD Faces Intensifying Competition From AMZN

TTD 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Buy rating, the bears have proven my bullish thesis wrong indeed, with TTD breaching its 5Y established floor at the $41s ranges as discussed in my last article and the stock already hitting a new 52-week low of $38s by the time of writing.

This development may potentially be attributed to the market's mounting uncertainties surrounding its future ad-tech prospects, attributed to the commencement of Amazon DSP's (AMZN) partnership with its streaming partners, including:

  • The Walt Disney Company (DIS) from Q3'25 onwards,
  • exclusive partnership - Roku (ROKU) from Q4'25 onwards, and
  • Netflix (NFLX) from Q4'25 onwards.

At a time when AMZN already reports outsized FQ3'25 advertising growth by +23.5% YoY against the tougher comparison from a year ago at +18.8% YoY, it is unsurprising that TTD has further retraced post FQ3'25 earnings call, attributed to its notably decelerating growth profile at +17.6% YoY and +27.3% YoY, respectively.

This development is unsurprising indeed, since AMZN now boasts an expanded exposure to the CTV advertising end market across four of its partners (including Prime Video) at an estimated 26% of the total TV viewing share and 56.8% of the total streaming share as of October 2025.

I am of the opinion that AMZN's growing DSP partnerships are likely to trigger headwinds to TTD's future ad-tech/CTV monetization cadence, attributed to the potentially reduced ad inventory and the narrowed advertiser base, since AMZN and ROKU comprise "more than 80% of U.S. CTV households according to ComScore data."

This is on top of AMZN's extremely aggressive pricing approach, as they reportedly "charges 0% for programmatic guaranteed deals on their owned media and 1% on open-web buys, far below TTD’s estimated 20% take rate" worsened by AMZN's "deepening partnership with Omnicom following its US media account win. Amazon is positioned as the most credible challenger TTD has faced in years."

The pricing headwind builds upon TTD's system issues as advertisers reportedly highlighted that "Kokai is less stable and more complex than Solimar—problems magnified during a quarter (Q4'25 holiday season) where execution errors carry outsized cost," attributed to numerous bugs such as "failed launches and missing audiences to broken integrations."

Given the higher outlays and the operational issues for the Kokai transition, it is unsurprising that TTD is currently at a great disadvantage, as Alphabet (GOOG) also "matches Amazon’s close-to-zero rates" while seemingly triggering the ongoing "race to the bottom," attributed to the former's narrower ad-tech operations/capabilities as an independent player against the deep-pocketed ad-tech giants.

Worsening the ongoing execution/industry/demand headwinds, TTD has further fed the pessimism with an underwhelming FQ4'25 revenue guidance of $840M (+13.3% YoY) against a year ago at +22.2% YoY, with it underscoring its painfully decelerating growth profile.

Assuming a further erosion in its advertiser base moving forward, I am of the opinion that TTD may report an underwhelming FY2026 performance as more of its advertisers migrate to its competitors' walled garden platforms.

TTD's Reversal Still Possible

On the other hand, it is important to note that eMarketer already expects CTV ad sales to grow from $28.79B in 2024 to $46.89B in 2028, expanding at a CAGR of +12.9% - with it underscoring an increasingly larger TAM for an expanding group of ad-tech players.

Given that "98.4% of those dollars go to video ads, solidifying its place as one of the fastest-growing major ad channels in the US," I am of the opinion that TTD may remain somewhat relevant despite the challenging market environment.

This is especially true as the management has reported a robust customer retention rate at "over 95% during the third quarter, as it has for the past 11 consecutive years," with it implying minimal erosion risks to its ad-tech prospects as of FQ3'25.

While it remains to be seen how FQ4'25 may develop as AMZN's DSP partnerships ramp up, market reports are already highlighting TTD's increasingly leaning on pricing negotiations "with 1% to 2% reductions or post-auction discounts available if agencies hit quarterly spend thresholds."

This development implies the management's willingness to engage with its advertiser base despite the risks of a sequentially impacted top/bottom-line performance, as advertisers seemingly gain "more leverage at a time when DSP fees are under heightened scrutiny."

At the same time, TTD has highlighted how 85% of its client base has transitioned from Solimar to Kokai, as the new platform also drives improved acquisition trends with lower costs - with it directly contrasting bearish market reports:

One of the most influential data analytics companies in the world recently ran a competitive test between The Trade Desk and a major walled garden, which offered to run campaigns with 0 fees. But because we're objective, data-driven and focused on measurable outcomes, we won the opportunity, securing about $20 million more in incremental spend through the end of 2025. (Seeking Alpha)

TTD's Cheap Valuations Present Two Stories

The Consensus Forward Estimates

The consensus forward estimates appear rather promising for now, with TTD still expected to report a decent top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +16.6%/+14.1% through FY2027, despite the notable deceleration from the 5Y historical growth profile at +29.9%/+35.1%, respectively.

TTD Valuations

Most importantly, TTD's maturing growth cadence has already been priced into its consistently discounted FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 21.73x, compared to the last Buy article levels of 29.06x, the 1Y mean of 39.42x, and the 5Y mean of 71.51x.

A similar trend may be observed in the reasonably valued estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.54x, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 21.73x and the consensus 3Y adj EPS estimates of +14.1%, compared to the 5Y mean of 2.59x.

When comparing TTD's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 21.73x and the estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.54x to its advertising/ad-tech peers, including:

  • DSP/SSP - Meta (META) at 26.34x/2.25x,
  • DSP/SSP - AMZN at 32.51x/1.62x,
  • DSP/SSP - GOOG at 30.25x/1.78x,
  • DSP/SSP - Unity (U) at 52.94x/9.98x,
  • DSP/SSP - AppLovin (APP) at 65.55x/1.93x, and
  • SSP - Magnite (MGNI) at 16.40x/1.18x, respectively,

it is apparent that TTD offers a compelling value Buy opportunity if its existing advertiser base stays on its ad-tech platform and eventually triggers its renewed profitable growth trends, as per consensus forward estimates.

Otherwise, it goes without saying that the stock's cheap valuations may present a potential value trap if the departure of its advertiser base proves permanent and their performance metrics are underwhelming compared to expectations/estimates.

Only time may tell.

TTD Downgraded To A Hold

TTD 5Y Stock Price

It goes without saying that TTD's 5Y price chart appears extremely brutal, with the stock losing all of its gains while notably breaching the established 5Y floor of $41s by the time of writing.

TTD Technical Indicators

It remains to be seen when a bounce may occur as well, with TTD's oversold RSIs and the moderating trading volumes implying a further deterioration in its stock price momentum pending any positive catalysts.

It goes without saying that the speculative stock price recovery directly hinges on Kokai's ability to retain its advertiser base without overly diluting the profit margins, barring which, the stock may potentially further retrace from current levels and retest the February 2020 peak of $30s in the near-term.

Pending further clarity on TTD's performance in the upcoming FQ4'25 earnings call sometime in February 2026, I am of the opinion that the stock may remain rather volatile in the near term, with the consistently moderating valuations and the loss of its 5Y stock price gains highlighting the inherent lack of bullish support worsened by the elevated short interest ratio of 11.05%.

Combined with the underwhelming outcome to my proven-to-be-wrong buy calls over the past three Buy articles, I am downgrading the stock to a Hold here.

It may be more prudent to observe from the sidelines for now.
